source.title: UMI-linked consensus sequencing enables phylogenetic analysis of directed evolution

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper addresses the challenge of obtaining accurate full-length sequences of gene variants from directed evolution libraries to study epistasis. Existing methods either lack throughput (Sanger), cannot capture long-range mutations (Illumina short reads), or suffer from high error rates (nanopore long reads). The key insight is that UMI-tagging can link raw nanopore reads to their original template molecules, enabling consensus generation to overcome high error rates and achieve single-base precision.

section_gists.heading: UMI-linked consensus sequencing enables phylogenetic analysis of directed evolution
section_gists.gist: Introduces the problem: understanding epistasis in protein evolution requires accurate full-length sequences, but current sequencing methods are limited. Sanger is low-throughput, Illumina short reads cannot link distant mutations, and nanopore long reads have high error rates. Proposes UMIC-seq: UMI-tagging of gene variants allows assignment of raw nanopore reads to templates, enabling consensus generation for high accuracy. The method is demonstrated on an amine dehydrogenase directed evolution campaign.
process_signals: Motivation: need to map evolutionary trajectories with full-length sequences to study epistasis.
Constraint: nanopore error rates (85-95% accuracy) prevent single-point mutation assignment.
Hypothesis: UMI-linked consensus sequencing can overcome nanopore errors.
Design rationale: UMIs allow read-to-template assignment, enabling consensus polishing.
failures_or_limits: Synthetic long-read methods suffer from uneven coverage or high chimera rates.
Nanopore raw reads cannot be assigned to templates in closely related libraries.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Describes UMIC-seq workflow: UMI-tagging via two PCR cycles, transformation to reduce complexity, nanopore sequencing, and bioinformatic clustering/consensus. Simulations guided UMI length choice (50 bp). Benchmarking on defined mixtures showed >99.99% consensus accuracy, 98% mutation recall, and low error rates (0.008% per-base) at 35x coverage. Applied to three rounds of amine dehydrogenase evolution, reconstructing phylogenies and identifying epistatic interactions.
process_signals: Design choice: 50 bp UMIs based on simulations of clustering efficiency.
Key innovation: transformation step to selectively amplify a subset of UMI-variant combinations, avoiding PCR bias.
Validation: 98% of true mutations identified; false negatives due to clustering errors.
Application: phylogenetic reconstruction of directed evolution lineages, revealing sign epistasis.
methods: Gibson assembly and transformation for complexity reduction
Filtering mutations by read support fraction (>60%)
failures_or_limits: False negatives likely due to clustering errors (mixed clusters).
Need for filtering to remove systematic errors (e.g., indels).

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Highlights advantages: low cost (<$1.1 per mutant vs. $5 Sanger), uniform coverage, scalability, and simplicity. Compares favorably to synthetic long-read methods which struggle with amplicon libraries. Notes that current epistasis studies are limited to short domains; UMIC-seq enables full-length analysis. Suggests integration with deep mutational scanning (DMS) for fitness measurements. Acknowledges limitations: UMI diversity limits library size, and highly diverse libraries may need reference assembly.
process_signals: Uniform coverage over gene length, unlike synthetic long reads.
Simplicity: only Gibson assembly and one PCR, benchtop sequencer.
Limitation: UMI diversity caps library size (~1 million variants).
Future direction: combine with DMS for full-protein fitness landscapes.
metrics: Throughput: ~100,000 consensus sequences per MinION flow cell
failures_or_limits: UMI diversity limits library size to ~1 million variants.
Highly diverse libraries may require reference assembly before polishing.
Current epistasis studies limited to short domains; full-length analysis needed.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Details experimental procedures: random mutagenesis of amine dehydrogenase, directed evolution by absorbance-activated droplet sorting (AADS), sequencing library preparation (UMI-tagging with 50 bp UMIs, Gibson assembly, transformation, plasmid isolation, nanopore sequencing), and bioinformatic pipeline (basecalling, UMI extraction, clustering, consensus generation with nanopolish, filtering).
process_signals: UMI design: two 25N stretches (total 50 bp) in reverse primer.
Library preparation: two PCR cycles to tag 500 ng input DNA.
Transformation: ~3000 colonies per round to oversample ~1000 variants.
Sequencing target: 100-fold sample coverage (>1.1 Gb for 9000 fragments).
Bioinformatics: greedy clustering, nanopolish variant calling, filtering by read support.
methods: AADS sorting (top 1000 variants from ~1 million droplets)
UMI-tagging by two-cycle PCR with barcoded primers
failures_or_limits: PCR bias minimized by low cycle number, but not eliminated.
Clustering algorithm may produce mixed clusters, causing false negatives.
Requires high sequencing coverage (100-fold sample coverage).

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The paper follows a typical technology development arc: identify a bottleneck (inability to get accurate long reads for directed evolution libraries), propose a solution (UMI-tagging + nanopore consensus), validate with benchmarks and real data, and discuss broader implications.
The transformation step is a key innovation to control molecule count and reduce PCR bias, distinguishing UMIC-seq from other UMI-based methods.
The phylogenetic analysis of directed evolution serves as a compelling application to demonstrate the method's value for studying epistasis.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Exact UMI collision rates and their impact on clustering accuracy (mentioned simulations but not detailed in these sections).
Performance on libraries with higher diversity (e.g., metagenomics) beyond the stated limit of ~1 million variants.
Quantitative comparison of PCR bias vs. transformation bias.
Scalability to larger genes or more complex libraries.
Details of the clustering algorithm's efficiency and error modes.

events.summary: In directed evolution libraries, closely related variants cannot be distinguished by raw nanopore reads, making consensus generation impossible.
UMI-tagging can link nanopore reads to their original template molecules, enabling accurate consensus generation.
Transformation step used to selectively amplify a subset of UMI-variant combinations, reducing complexity and bias.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk introduces the problem, hypothesis, design choices, computational method, and validation metrics for UMIC-seq.

events.summary: Directed evolution of amine dehydrogenase (AmDH) was performed using error-prone PCR and absorbance-activated droplet sorting (AADS) over three rounds.
Hot-spot analysis revealed mutation accumulation near the substrate binding pocket and distal positions, emphasizing the importance of long-range mutations.
Phylogenetic analysis using sequence similarity networks identified founder variants and epistatic interactions, enabled by long-read sequencing.
Founder variants and their constituent mutations were regenerated and tested in lysate activity assays to verify beneficial effects and epistasis.
numbers_or_metrics: A64E R102S E323V increases activity to 154 ± 33% of A64E R102S

events.summary: Sign epistasis was detected between mutations A64E, R102S, and E323V, spanning 780 bp, demonstrating long-range interactions only identifiable with long reads.
Characterization of the founder variant A64E R102S E323V revealed altered catalytic properties, including increased kcat and KM, and compensation of deleterious expression effects.
numbers_or_metrics: A64E R102S E323V total turnover number 65,900 ± 380

events.summary: Conventional short-read sequencing would have misannotated E323V as an activating mutation, highlighting the limitation of hot-spot analysis without long-range linkage.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk describes the application of UMIC-seq to directed evolution of AmDH, including phylogenetic analysis, identification of epistasis, and experimental validation. It provides key results demonstrating the method's utility.

events.summary: Analysis of epistatic interactions: mutation E323V is individually deleterious but compensated by A64E, and combined mutations improve catalytic efficiency.
Validation of improved variant in biocatalytic assay: variant A64E R102S E323V outperforms wild type and A64E in total turnover number.
Cost and throughput analysis: UMIC-seq generates ~100,000 consensus sequences at <$1.1 per mutant, 450-fold cheaper than Sanger.
Limitation of synthetic long-read methods: uneven coverage, high chimeric reads, and low full-length read percentages for amplicon sequencing.
Limitation of current epistasis studies: they focus on short domains, missing complete epistatic interactions in entire proteins.
Design advantage of UMIC-seq: simple workflow with Gibson assembly and one PCR, uniform coverage, and benchtop sequencer.
Hypothesis for future integration: combining UMIC-seq with deep mutational scanning (DMS) could enable full-protein fitness landscapes and structure determination.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains analysis of epistatic interactions, validation of improved variant, limitations of short-read and synthetic long-read methods, cost analysis, design advantages, and future hypothesis for DMS integration.

events.summary: Enzyme purification and characterization protocol described for amine dehydrogenase variants.
Data and code availability statements indicate deposition of sequencing data and release of analysis scripts.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk mainly contains detailed experimental protocols, data/code availability, and references. It does not introduce new research evolution events beyond standard methodological descriptions. The only process-related content is the enzyme characterization protocol, which is a routine experimental step rather than a novel design or problem-solving event.

evidence_bank.quote: An erroneous raw third-generation sequencing read cannot reliably be assigned to its template, when multiple template candidates differing only in few point mutations are possible. This inability to assign reads to any one parental molecule therefore renders consensus-based “polishing” from unlinked reads impossible.

we leverage UMI-tags to assign erroneous nanopore reads to their molecule of origin, facilitating clustering for accurate consensus formation even when starting with a pool of highly similar sequences

Uniquely, the UMI-variant complexity is reduced by a transformation step into cells, because an exact number of molecules is represented in the number of transformant colonies, directly combining selective amplification and control over molecule count in a straightforward molecular biology step.
1-3 mutations were introduced per gene and per round of directed evolution using error-prone PCR

A hot-spot analysis based on the frequency of mutation during the experimental directed evolution not only revealed several positions randomly scattered over the full length of the protein, but also indicated accumulation close to the loop covering the substrate binding pocket
The sequence similarity graph clusters the sequences of mutants selected in each round of evolution into a phylogenetic structure
epistatically interacting mutations can be derived in this workflow based on information about co-occurrence of mutations
variants were regenerated: point mutated primers were incorporated in whole-plasmid PCR, in a straightforward cloning strategy termed IVA cloning
All mutations in the core sets were tested individually and in combination in a lysate activity assay
an example of sign epistasis can be seen in the founder variants A64E R102S E323V
the epistatic interaction extends across 780 bp (or 260 amino acids) in the sequence
the distance of at least 18 Å between the mutations that are shown to interact epistatically
The decrease in protein expression caused by mutation E323V, however, is compensated by mutation A64E, laying the foundation of the epistatic interactions

With conventional short-read sequencing for hot-spot analysis, E323V would have wrongly been determined as an activating mutation; yet E323V is only beneficial in the context of other mutations, which was correctly detected by long-read sequencing
Mutation E323V, on the other hand, individually is deleterious in the lysate assay (Fig. 4b), stemming from a reduced catalytic efficiency as well as a stark reduction in expression (Table 1).
The decrease in protein expression caused by mutation E323V, however, is compensated by mutation A64E, laying the foundation of the epistatic interactions.
When E323V is combined with the other mutations, a decreased $K_{M}$ value results in higher catalytic efficiencies compared to mutation A64E alone
This increased performance is confirmed in a biocatalytic assay, the determination of the enzyme's total turnover number.
the variant A64E R102S E323V again outperforms not only the wild type but also mutation A64E by a wide margin
our workflow can generate approximately 100,000 accurate consensus sequences at 50-fold coverage.
This entails a cost per mutant sequence of $<1.1\Phi$ , a 450-fold reduction compared to around 5 USD using classical Sanger sequencing
Uneven coverage over the full length of the parent fragment results in these methods needing high sequencing coverage and still often failing at generating full-length consensus sequences.
Capturing the entire protein sequence would be necessary to reveal the complete, presumably more complex epistatic interactions in entire proteins rather than domains.
the tagged sequencing library is obtained based on straightforward steps that only involve Gibson assembly and one PCR.
Only a benchtop nanopore sequencer is required and the entire procedure can be carried out by one laboratory operator
A synthesis of DMS and UMIC-seq would start with sequencing of the naïve library using the UMIC-seq workflow, resulting in accurate full-length sequences and their corresponding UMIs.
In a second step, short-read sequencing of only the UMI-tag would suffice to determine the frequencies of variants after a functional assay, deriving fitness scores
Proteins were purified from cleared cell lysate via affinity chromatography using Step-Tactin Sepharose (IBA Lifesciences), according to the manufacturer's instructions.
Sequencing data generated in this study are deposited in the European Nucleotide Archive under the accession code PRJEB35468.
All scripts used for the UMIC-seq workflow and an analysis of example data are available at https://github.com/fhlab/UMIC-seq

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Obtaining accurate full-length sequences of gene variants from directed evolution libraries to study epistasis is challenging because existing methods lack throughput, cannot link distant mutations, or have high error rates.

canonical_events.summary: In directed evolution libraries, closely related variants cannot be distinguished by raw nanopore reads, making consensus generation impossible.
UMI-tagging can link nanopore reads to their original template molecules, enabling accurate consensus generation.
Transformation step used to selectively amplify a subset of UMI-variant combinations, reducing complexity and bias.
Directed evolution of amine dehydrogenase (AmDH) was performed using error-prone PCR and absorbance-activated droplet sorting (AADS) over three rounds.
Hot-spot analysis revealed mutation accumulation near the substrate binding pocket and distal positions, emphasizing the importance of long-range mutations.
Phylogenetic analysis using sequence similarity networks identified founder variants and epistatic interactions, enabled by long-read sequencing.
Founder variants and their constituent mutations were regenerated and tested in lysate activity assays to verify beneficial effects and epistasis.
key_numbers: A64E R102S E323V increases activity to 154 ± 33% of A64E R102S

canonical_events.summary: Sign epistasis was detected between mutations A64E, R102S, and E323V, spanning 780 bp, demonstrating long-range interactions only identifiable with long reads.
Characterization of the founder variant A64E R102S E323V revealed altered catalytic properties, including increased kcat and KM, and compensation of deleterious expression effects.
key_numbers: A64E R102S E323V total turnover number 65,900 ± 380

canonical_events.summary: Conventional short-read sequencing would have misannotated E323V as an activating mutation, highlighting the limitation of hot-spot analysis without long-range linkage.
Validation of improved variant in biocatalytic assay: variant A64E R102S E323V outperforms wild type and A64E in total turnover number.
Cost and throughput analysis: UMIC-seq generates ~100,000 consensus sequences at <$1.1 per mutant, 450-fold cheaper than Sanger.
Limitation of synthetic long-read methods: uneven coverage, high chimeric reads, and low full-length read percentages for amplicon sequencing.
Limitation of current epistasis studies: they focus on short domains, missing complete epistatic interactions in entire proteins.

edges.reason: The problem of unassignable reads motivates the hypothesis that UMI-tagging can enable read-to-template assignment.
The hypothesis leads to the design of a transformation step to control UMI-variant complexity.
The design of UMIC-seq enables its application to the directed evolution experiment.
After the directed evolution experiment, hot-spot analysis is performed on the resulting sequences.
Hot-spot analysis reveals mutation patterns that are further investigated through phylogenetic analysis.
Phylogenetic analysis identifies founder variants, which are then regenerated and tested.
Lysate assay results reveal sign epistasis, which is analyzed in detail.
The detection of epistasis motivates deeper characterization of the founder variant.
Characterization highlights the limitation of short-read methods, which would misannotate E323V.
The limitation of short-read methods underscores the value of UMIC-seq, validated by biocatalytic assay.
After validation, the cost and throughput advantages of UMIC-seq are analyzed.
Cost analysis leads to comparison with synthetic long-read methods, highlighting their limitations.
Discussion of method limitations extends to the broader limitation of current epistasis studies.

must_cover_points: Problem of unassignable nanopore reads in directed evolution libraries
Hypothesis that UMI-tagging enables consensus generation
Design of transformation step to reduce complexity
Phylogenetic analysis identifying founder variants and epistasis
Regeneration and lysate assay of founder variants
Detection of sign epistasis across 780 bp
Characterization of catalytic properties and expression compensation
Limitation of short-read sequencing for epistasis detection
Cost and throughput advantages of UMIC-seq
